column2: The trees on both sides kept changing: now pines, now birches, now oaks.
A wistful feeling came over them.
She remembered how many trees had been planted.
And then — how many of them had had to be uprooted, cleared away, torn out.
They got in the way of walking, wild strawberries grew under them and you couldn't get to them, but it was very hard to be rid of them.
The trees had won in the end, and now the people lived in their yard, winning back for themselves a little space amid the thickets and the windfall.
Lilichka knew that this was exactly what Miyechka had remembered, and so she turned on the radio and stopped her beloved chatter with the traveler.
Until he started talking himself:
— Now in the old days, — he began, — in the old days there was none of this.
— There wasn't, — Lilichka nodded.
— And now our whole government keeps sending plague down on the pigs.
— Really?
— Lilichka turned around and looked at the passenger.
Some French rap was playing on the radio.
— Yeah.
One of mine dropped dead.
— And how do they send it?
— asked Miyechka, turning the radio down.
— They spray something in the air, — said the man and showed with his fingers how.
— And what do they need that for?
— If only I knew, girls, — sighed the man.
— They say it like this: look for who profits from it.
— And do they profit?
— Miyechka inquired.
— Very much so!
— the man snapped.
— It's like this: the worse it is for us, the better it is for them.
He wanted to keep the conversation going, but his arguments ran out, and he sat there sadly.
The man got out at the turn.
He tried to give them money, then tossed two apples onto the back seat and went off home through the apple orchard.
— Miyechka, — asked Lilichka, — and is this the same apple orchard?
— The very same, — said Miyechka.
— And who does it belong to now?
— To us.
— To us?
— Well, to Mom.
— And who takes care of it?
— No one, really, — said Miyechka.
— Grandfather, you see, had this idea of the common good, that everyone would eat apples, tend the orchard together and have a good laugh.
Instead, we have this.
They looked out the window at the orchard.
It was a whole field planted with apple trees.
Between them tall grass and thistles had grown up.
No one cleared the orchard; young apple saplings had seeded the rows in between.
When the orchard wasn't yet so overgrown, a truck used to come here and people shook down the apples to turn them in for juice.
Now the apple trees could no longer be reached.
— Miyechka, — said Lilya, — don't you dare take on these apple-tree thickets.
Miyechka got out of the car, leaned against its sun-warmed side and looked at the apple trees that grew all the way to the horizon.
— Solomiya, — said Lilya, standing beside her sister, — don't even think about it.
— To your grandfather, — said the grandmother, once they had arrived and were sitting at the table, — an apple tree was like a cow in India — a sacred animal.
All he ever did was come up with these arguments.
First his mother loved apples — though I know perfectly well that his mother never once put an apple to her lips, because they gave her cramps.
Then he'd say that apples are very good for you.
That's true, of course, but is that any reason to plant eight hundred of the things?
— He had a right to it, — said Marta, holding Lusya on one knee, because her belly got in the way of using both.
Lusya was banging a doll's head against the table.
— Well, and who says he didn't?
— said the grandmother.
— It's just that he liked to be certain of what he was doing, and he didn't like it when not everyone happily agreed with him.
Now let me tell you — when he announced that we were going to plant a communal orchard, no one came.
He toiled there alone for days on end.
Then they said they'd pay for planting the trees — and somebody came, but they planted them so badly it would have been better not to plant them at all.
I myself, do you know how much water I carried there?
And how many lunches I cooked?
And for the sake of what?
— Granny, you're saying it in a way, — Miyechka cut in, — that casts doubt on grandfather's saintliness.
— You know yourselves that he was no saint, — the grandmother laughed, — because no one is.
But I signed myself up for eternal service to great ideas.

First I served the idea of a great state and spent thirty years on it, and then I served his ideas, and now, when there's very little use left in my service, simply because of my age, only now do I have the right to serve no one but my own ideas.
— And how is it?
— asked Lilichka.
— Wonderful, — the grandmother spoke up.
— Only there aren't any ideas left.
Lilichka sat behind the screen, editing the photos of the pumpkins.
— Liliya, — said the grandmother, — and that, what's-his-name, who brought us this blond little angel, has he been in touch?
— Who?
— asked Lilichka.
— Lilya, don't be slow, the little angel is sitting right here, — Marta spoke up.
— And understands everything, — Miyechka nodded.
— He hasn't been in touch...
— replied Lilichka.
— So is she ours now for good?
The police won't come looking for us?
Lusya parted her lips and listened attentively.
— I'll call him, — said Lilichka.
And indeed, when evening came, she called Ivan.
He picked up and immediately showered her with apologies.
— What are you apologizing for?
— asked Lilichka.
— I didn't want to tell you, — he began, — but Natalya...
she's been gone for half a year now… Since February.
— Are you an idiot?
— Lilichka cried out.
She stood in the dark garden, so that no one would hear her conversation, and most of all so as to be far from the child, who was peacefully snuffling just then in the grandmother's bed.

— At first I thought I'd manage on my own, and then I gave her to my parents, but Mom can't anymore, she can barely walk, and Natalya's mother has been gone since Natalya was seventeen, — Ivan made excuses.
— Listen, — Lilichka interrupted him, — I really am sorry that you've ended up in such a situation, but this isn't a little dog you've put into boarding, this is a human being!
— I know, Lilichka, but Natalya begged me so, I told you, — Ivan made excuses.
— It's just that I'm abroad right now, I have work here…
— You ran away?
— Lilichka said in a hushed voice, looked at the phone screen and ended the call.
He tried to call again and texted her in the messenger, but everything was clear.
The next morning she called Ivan's mother.
The woman straight off began thanking her for agreeing to take the child.
— You know him better than I do, — the woman said. — He can't be a father.
— What do you mean, he can't be a father?
He already is a father!
And twice over, at that!
— Lilichka couldn't restrain herself.
— Well, think for yourself, how is a man supposed to raise a daughter alone, the poor little orphan?
— Lusya is not an orphan, — retorted Lilichka.
She came up to the table, which was already set for lunch.
The grandmother had made potatoes by her own recipe — in their skins with caramelized onion.
Marta stood whisking oil with vinegar to dress the salad.
— We have a girl, — said Lilichka.
And burst into tears.
That same day Marta went into town and bought all sorts of things for Lusya.
Striped T-shirts, shorts, dresses, a doll's pram and, indeed, the dolls themselves, since up to then Lusya had been playing with the very ones the three sisters had played with in childhood.
Marta's dolls had their eyes gouged out and their mouths cut open, so they could be given water to drink, which then leaked out of holes in their heads.
Practically all the dolls were bald — the ones whose hair was inserted into a slit on the forehead had lost it for understandable reasons.
The rest of the dolls Lilya had cut in several stages, having decided at ten that she would be a hairdresser.
Besides that, Marta bought Lusya bedding with blue polka dots and two pairs of shoes.
Together with the little girl they arranged it all in the far little room, adjoining Marta's bedroom.
They washed the window, the floor and the walls, moved in there the little chest of drawers that stood by the bed where Lilichka and Miyechka slept, and put away the new clothes.
Lusya was no little angel and sometimes squealed like a piglet.
— She'll sleep here, — said Marta. — I don't sleep at night anyway, so I'll keep an eye on her.
Maria
The following week Maria returned from her sea voyage.
She didn't come to the raspberry thickets right away.
First — home, where she sat for a while on the potentially life-threatening little balcony.
She took a breath.
Drank some coffee.
Swept away the dust.
She looked around, her gaze lingering on the large photo of her mother's parents — they were leaning their heads toward each other.
Her grandmother had delicately painted lips.

Something about her recalled Miyechka — whether it was her bearing or her high forehead, which back then it wasn't the custom to cover, and every time Solomiya came out of the bathroom with her hair combed back, the grandmother would say that she had the signature high forehead of her own mother.
Maria wanted to see both of her daughters — that hadn't happened in several years now, but something was gnawing at her.
From her very childhood she had tried to separate herself from her parents as much as possible, and demanded the same of her own daughters.
'Farther apart in deed,' she would say, 'closer in heart.'

On top of that, every corner of that house and yard loomed over her with responsibility — it seemed to her that it was she herself who was to blame for the rotted terrace, for the lost battle with the bushes and trees.
And, despite the feeling of her own guilt, she didn't want to take responsibility for it.
This feeling didn't leave her even when she stood at the gate.
It opened with difficulty, because relentless hops and ivy had crept over its corners.
Both daughters came out of the bushes somewhere with Marta; she hugged her own, and then went up to Marta with a smile.
— Maria, — said Marta, — just let's do without all of that.
— Marta, I'm not doing anything, — said Maria with a broad smile.
— But how it suits you!
Marta hugged her aunt lightly, not touching her with her belly.
Maria was over sixty — she wore pale, peach-colored trousers and a white linen shirt.
You couldn't tell that she made any great effort to look good, and yet she gave an impression of lightness and confidence.
Looking at her, no one ever felt awkward over beauty that had withered.
— My queen Teodora, — said Maria, turning to her mother.
— Just think: our whole family together!
The whole female part of it, while all those who bring their joy and trouble have drifted off somewhere.
There are five of us, together we can move mountains.
— Or we can just make lunch, — said the grandmother.
— Or not make it, even, — Miyechka laughed.
— That's exactly the happiness of five women.
— Six, — Lilichka corrected her.
Lilichka and Maria stood together by the sofa in the living room, on which, arms flung out, lay Lusya, worn out by the summer, asleep.
She was naked, covered only by the light sky-blue scarf that Lilichka had recently used to cover herself at the tire shop.
— God, — Maria whispered.
In this room you could feel even more how she smelled of freshness and a barely perceptible scent of perfume.
Maria blinked her slightly sun-faded but still long lashes.
Lusya's straight, thin hair lay on her shoulders.
The little hairs that touched her body were slightly darker — they stuck to her from the heat.
The little girl had almost no eyebrows, and her lashes sometimes quivered like cobwebs.
— God, — Maria repeated.
— Whose happiness is this, Lilichka?
— I don't know, Mom, sort of mine, — she retorted.

When Marta and Lusya had fallen asleep for their afternoon nap, and Lilichka and Miyechka had driven off to look at the orchard, Maria and the grandmother sat on the old log and looked out over the walnut valley.
No one gathered the walnuts in it.
They grew, fell, and sprouted again.
— Well, Mom, tell me, — Maria spoke up.
— Both of your daughters have come, — the grandmother began.
— Well, I can see that, — said Maria.
— Miyechka is suffering.
— That I know.
For long?
— No, on and off.
— And Lilichka?
— Lilichka doesn't want to go to Australia.
— And does she have to?
— asked Maria.
— Well, that's for her to decide.
To just up and fly off like that?
I don't know whether she should.
Better if they stayed close.
Men come and go, but there are only the two of them, better if they stayed side by side.
— And is that for certain now?
— Is what for certain?
